briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for May 25, 2026. Today, we will cover the escalating impacts of the global climate crisis, calls for urgent policy action and corporate accountability, and the current status of the renewable energy transition.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Climate Crisis and Immediate Impacts
sections.content: The impacts of climate change are intensifying worldwide. Rivers in the Himalayan region are becoming more turbulent, leading to a surge in flood and erosion risks [1], while the reduction of Arctic ice is contributing to record-breaking heatwaves in Europe [3]. Snowfall in the Greek mountains has decreased by more than half in 40 years [5], the UK saw its May temperature records broken [48], and the U.S. state of Michigan experienced 33 tornadoes and severe flooding last year alone [54]. These phenomena are exacerbated by scientific analyses indicating that climate change is worsening summer air quality, directly impacting our lungs [36, 37], and even lengthening Earth's day [41]. Furthermore, research reports [46, 47] highlight that climate change is causing immense damage to jobs and businesses and devastating rural Bhutan, underscoring the severity of the situation.

sections.title: ⚠️ Policy Delays and Corporate Evasion of Responsibility: Obstacles on the Path to Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Policy and corporate efforts to address the climate crisis remain insufficient or are even regressing. Domestically, the National Assembly's amendment to the Carbon Neutrality Act has been postponed yet again, breaking the promise of 'May processing' [9, 16]. There are calls for urgent development of realistic strategies amidst the dilemma between achieving AI powerhouse status and carbon neutrality [8]. Internationally, leaked documents have revealed that BHP, the world's largest mining company, is freezing key projects and retreating from climate action [43, 50, 52, 53]. It has even been found to be purchasing hundreds of millions of dollars worth of diesel trucks, in violation of its own climate strategy [51]. The UN Environment Programme (UNEP) has warned that decarbonization in the building and construction sector is slowing down despite improvements in efficiency [45]. Furthermore, China's new carbon measurement methods are creating a Germany-sized gap in reported emissions, raising international concerns [55].

sections.title: 🌱 Hope and Challenges in the Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Renewable energy is emerging as a core strategy to save the planet and safeguard national security, projected to become the dominant force in the electricity market [20, 21, 25, 31]. By 2040, renewables like solar and wind are expected to grow into the primary drivers of the power market [20], and banks are also shifting their funding from real estate to AI and renewable energy sectors [28]. Pumped-hydro storage is gaining attention as a crucial 'battery' for the renewable energy era [30]. Gyeongsangnam-do Province is pioneering a resident-led solar economy by launching the nation's first 'Solar Renewable Energy Comprehensive Service Company (ReSCO) Market' [26]. However, significant challenges remain, including a clear regional imbalance in renewable energy self-sufficiency rates [23], concerns that the abolition of REC weighting will directly hit offshore wind development [24], and the formation of a farmers' committee to address damages caused by solar power projects [29].

sections.title: 🇰🇷🌍 Climate Response Efforts by Communities and Ecosystems
sections.content: Communities and ecosystems are responding to climate change in various ways. Incheon City is making all-out efforts to prevent disaster damage to farmers and fishermen amidst climate change [2, 4]. Ulsan University and the University of Dundee in the UK are promoting educational cooperation for climate crisis response [6]. Seongdong-gu has achieved excellent results in carbon neutrality and climate crisis response [12]. Geoje City and Dongdaemun-gu are each spreading carbon neutrality practices in daily life through the operation of 'visiting carbon neutrality promotional booths' and environmental education weeks, respectively [14, 17]. Candidates for Superintendent of Education in Chungcheongbuk-do are presenting future visions with carbon neutrality as a key pledge [10, 11, 15, 19]. Seosan Seoil Middle School conducted a tree-planting event as part of its 'Creating a Carbon-Neutral School' initiative [18]. Internationally, Gentoo penguins are exhibiting adaptive behavior by advancing their breeding season to cope with heatwaves caused by climate change [38], and acquisitions of sustainable fashion brands are taking place [40], indicating ongoing efforts across society to address the climate crisis.

briefing.closing: As we have discussed in today's briefing, the impacts of the climate crisis are expanding globally, and it is imperative that policy and corporate responses accelerate. Simultaneously, adaptation and mitigation efforts by various communities and ecosystems continue to offer sustained hope. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Incheon's Eulwangri, Wangsan, and Hanagae Beaches to Open Simultaneously on the 20th of Next Month
articles.original_title: 인천 을왕리·왕산·하나개 해수욕장, 내달 20일 일제히 개장
articles.description: Citizens already enjoying water activities in early summer heat. On the 25th, with early summer heat continuing, citizens were seen enjoying water activities at Eulwangri Beach in Jung-gu, Incheon. Senior Reporter Jung Ji-yoon. Due to earlier-than-usual heat, Incheon's Eulwangri, Wangsan, and Hanagae Beaches will all open on June 20th. Jung-gu, Incheon, anticipates an early heatwave this year, leading to the early opening of three beaches: Eulwangri and Wangsan Beaches on Yongyudo Island, and Hanagae Beach on Muuido Island...

summary.phenomenon: On May 25th, citizens enjoyed water activities at Eulwangri Beach in Jung-gu, Incheon. Due to earlier-than-usual heat, Incheon's Eulwangri, Wangsan, and Hanagae Beaches are scheduled to open simultaneously on June 20th.
summary.cause: The article mentioned 'earlier-than-usual heat' and 'an early heatwave expected this year,' but a scientific explanation for the cause was not specified.
summary.outlook: The three beaches in Jung-gu, Incheon—Eulwangri, Wangsan, and Hanagae—are expected to open from June 20th, allowing citizens to enjoy water activities.

articles.original_title: Why Michigan is emerging as one of America’s worst-hit climate states
articles.description: The state saw 33 tornadoes last year and severe flooding as researchers say links to climate change are undeniableThe tornado hit west Ann Arbor at 1.45am on 15 April, passing through Veterans Memorial park, where it knocked several mature oak trees and ripped up baseball field fences before setting its sights on a local ice rink.“It came up through the parking lot and, in that time, the pressure differential between the tornado and the air inside the rink collapsed the wall,” said Scott Spooner

summary.phenomenon: Michigan experienced 33 tornadoes and severe flooding last year. For instance, a tornado that struck West Ann Arbor at 1:45 AM on April 15th caused damage to Veterans Memorial Park and a local ice rink.
summary.cause: Researchers state that the link between these phenomena and climate change is undeniable.
